questions.question: In which country is the Eiffel Tower located?
questions.explanation: The Eiffel Tower is located in Paris, France. It was built in 1889 and is one of the most visited monuments in the world.
questions.funFact: The Eiffel Tower grows about 15 centimetres taller in summer because the iron expands in the heat!

questions.question: What is the Great Wall of China famous for?
options: Being the longest wall ever built
questions.explanation: The Great Wall of China is the longest wall ever built, stretching over 21,000 kilometres across China.
questions.funFact: The Great Wall is so long that it would take about 18 months to walk from one end to the other!

questions.question: In which country are the Pyramids of Giza?
questions.explanation: The Pyramids of Giza are in Egypt. They were built as tombs for the ancient Egyptian pharaohs over 4,500 years ago.
questions.funFact: The Great Pyramid of Giza was the tallest building in the world for over 3,800 years!

questions.question: The Statue of Liberty is a famous monument in which country?
questions.explanation: The Statue of Liberty stands in New York City, USA. It was a gift from France to the United States in 1886.
questions.funFact: The Statue of Liberty wears a size 879 shoe! Her feet are over 7.5 metres long.

questions.question: What colour is the Taj Mahal?
questions.explanation: The Taj Mahal in Agra, India, is made of beautiful white marble. It was built by Emperor Shah Jahan in memory of his wife.
questions.funFact: The Taj Mahal changes colour throughout the day — pinkish in the morning, white in the afternoon, and golden at night!

questions.question: The Colosseum is an ancient arena located in which city?
questions.explanation: The Colosseum is in Rome, Italy. It was built nearly 2,000 years ago and could hold about 50,000 spectators.
questions.funFact: The Colosseum could be filled with water to stage mock sea battles!

questions.question: Which famous clock tower is in London, England?
questions.explanation: Big Ben is the famous clock tower at the Palace of Westminster in London. The name actually refers to the great bell inside the tower.
questions.funFact: Big Ben's clock is so accurate that a penny is placed on top of the pendulum to adjust the time when needed!

questions.question: Which tower in Italy is famous for leaning to one side?
questions.explanation: The Leaning Tower of Pisa in Italy started tilting during construction because the ground underneath was too soft on one side.
questions.funFact: The tower leans about 4 degrees to one side. Engineers worked for years to stop it from leaning further and falling over!

questions.question: The Sydney Opera House is in which country?
questions.explanation: The Sydney Opera House is in Sydney, Australia. Its roof looks like giant white sails or shells.
questions.funFact: The Sydney Opera House has over 1 million roof tiles, and they were all made in Sweden!

questions.question: What is the name of the giant statue of Jesus in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil?
questions.explanation: Christ the Redeemer is a huge statue of Jesus Christ that stands on top of Mount Corcovado in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil.
questions.funFact: Christ the Redeemer is 30 metres tall with arms stretching 28 metres wide — that is about as wide as a basketball court!

questions.question: Stonehenge is a circle of giant stones in which country?
questions.explanation: Stonehenge is in Wiltshire, England. It is a circle of huge standing stones that is about 5,000 years old.
questions.funFact: Nobody knows for certain why Stonehenge was built! Some think it was a calendar, a temple, or a place for healing.

questions.question: Which of these is the tallest building in the world?
questions.explanation: The Burj Khalifa in Dubai, UAE, is the tallest building in the world at 828 metres. That is more than twice the height of the Empire State Building!
questions.funFact: The Burj Khalifa is so tall that you can watch the sunset from the ground floor and then take the elevator to the top to watch it set again!

questions.question: The ancient city of Petra is carved into rock cliffs in which country?
questions.explanation: Petra is an ancient city in Jordan where buildings were carved directly into pink and red rock cliffs over 2,000 years ago.
questions.funFact: Petra is sometimes called the 'Rose City' because the rock it is carved from changes colour from pink to red throughout the day!

questions.question: What shape are the Egyptian Pyramids?
questions.explanation: The Egyptian Pyramids have a pyramid shape with a square base and four triangular sides that meet at a point on top.
questions.funFact: The ancient Egyptians built the pyramids without any modern machines — they used ramps, levers, and millions of stone blocks!

questions.question: The Statue of Liberty holds a torch in one hand. What does she hold in the other?
questions.explanation: The Statue of Liberty holds a tablet (book) in her left hand with the date July 4, 1776 — American Independence Day — written on it.
questions.funFact: The Statue of Liberty is green now, but when it was first built, it was shiny copper-brown like a new penny!

questions.question: Which monument has the face of a lion and the body of a lion, sitting near the Pyramids?
questions.explanation: The Great Sphinx of Giza has the body of a lion and the face of a human. It sits near the Pyramids in Egypt and is over 4,500 years old.
questions.funFact: The Sphinx's nose is missing! Nobody knows exactly how it was lost, but it has been missing for hundreds of years.

questions.question: Angkor Wat is the largest religious monument in the world. In which country is it?
questions.explanation: Angkor Wat is in Siem Reap, Cambodia. It was originally built as a Hindu temple in the 12th century.
questions.funFact: Angkor Wat appears on Cambodia's national flag — it is the only building in the world that appears on a country's flag!

questions.question: What is the Eiffel Tower made of?
questions.explanation: The Eiffel Tower is made of iron. It weighs about 7,300 tonnes and uses over 18,000 iron pieces held together by 2.5 million rivets.
questions.funFact: The Eiffel Tower is repainted every 7 years, and it takes about 60 tonnes of paint each time!

questions.question: The Forbidden City was the home of Chinese emperors. In which city is it?
questions.explanation: The Forbidden City is in Beijing, China. It was the palace of Chinese emperors for almost 500 years and has 980 buildings.
questions.funFact: The Forbidden City has 9,999 rooms! The number 9 was considered lucky by the Chinese emperors.

questions.question: Which ancient wonder had beautiful Hanging Gardens?
questions.explanation: The Hanging Gardens of Babylon were one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, believed to have been in ancient Babylon (modern-day Iraq).
questions.funFact: Some scientists think the Hanging Gardens might not have actually existed — they could be a legend passed down through stories!

questions.question: Machu Picchu is an ancient Inca city high in the mountains of which country?
questions.explanation: Machu Picchu is in the Andes Mountains of Peru. It was built by the Inca people around 1450 and sits at 2,430 metres above sea level.
questions.funFact: Machu Picchu was hidden from the outside world for hundreds of years until an American explorer named Hiram Bingham found it in 1911!

questions.question: The Parthenon is an ancient temple in which city?
questions.explanation: The Parthenon is on top of the Acropolis hill in Athens, Greece. It was built about 2,500 years ago as a temple to the goddess Athena.
questions.funFact: The Parthenon was once painted in bright colours — red, blue, and gold — but the paint wore off over thousands of years!

questions.question: Easter Island is famous for its giant stone statues called Moai. Where is Easter Island?
questions.explanation: Easter Island is a remote island in the southeastern Pacific Ocean, belonging to Chile. The Moai statues were carved by the Rapa Nui people.
questions.funFact: There are nearly 900 Moai statues on Easter Island, and the tallest one is about 10 metres high — as tall as a three-storey building!

questions.question: The Gateway of India is located in which Indian city?
questions.explanation: The Gateway of India is located in Mumbai, Maharashtra. It stands on the waterfront at Apollo Bunder, facing the Arabian Sea.
questions.funFact: The Gateway of India was built to welcome King George V and Queen Mary when they visited India in 1911!

questions.question: What is the name of the famous bridge with two towers in London?
questions.explanation: Tower Bridge is a famous bridge in London with two tall towers connected by walkways. It can open in the middle to let tall ships pass through.
questions.funFact: Tower Bridge opens about 800 times a year to let ships pass through!

questions.question: The Chichen Itza pyramid is an ancient Mayan monument in which country?
questions.explanation: Chichen Itza is in the Yucatan Peninsula of Mexico. The main pyramid, El Castillo, was built by the ancient Maya civilization around 1000 CE.
questions.funFact: During the spring and autumn equinoxes, shadows on El Castillo create the illusion of a serpent slithering down the pyramid steps!

questions.question: How tall is the Eiffel Tower?
questions.explanation: The Eiffel Tower is approximately 330 metres tall (including antennas). When it was built in 1889, it was the tallest structure in the world.
questions.funFact: Gustave Eiffel had a secret apartment at the very top of the tower where he entertained famous guests like Thomas Edison!

questions.question: The Acropolis of Athens contains which famous ancient temple?
questions.explanation: The Acropolis of Athens contains the Parthenon, a temple dedicated to the Greek goddess Athena. It was built between 447 and 432 BCE.
questions.funFact: The word 'Acropolis' means 'high city' in Greek because it sits on a rocky hill 150 metres above the city!

questions.question: Which country gifted the Statue of Liberty to the United States?
questions.explanation: France gifted the Statue of Liberty to the United States in 1886 as a symbol of friendship and shared values of freedom and democracy.
questions.funFact: The statue was shipped from France to America in 350 separate pieces packed in 214 crates, and it took 4 months to reassemble!

questions.question: The Great Wall of China was built primarily to protect against what?
questions.explanation: The Great Wall was built over many centuries to protect China from invasions by nomadic groups from the north, including the Mongols.
questions.funFact: The Great Wall is NOT visible from space with the naked eye, despite the popular myth — it is too narrow to see from orbit!

questions.question: Which pharaoh is the Great Pyramid of Giza believed to have been built for?
questions.explanation: The Great Pyramid of Giza was built for Pharaoh Khufu (also known as Cheops) around 2560 BCE. It took about 20 years to build.
questions.funFact: The Great Pyramid contains about 2.3 million stone blocks, each weighing an average of 2.5 tonnes — as heavy as a large car!

questions.question: What events took place in the Colosseum in ancient Rome?
questions.explanation: The Colosseum hosted gladiator fights, animal hunts, and other public spectacles. It could hold up to 50,000 spectators.
questions.funFact: The Colosseum had a huge retractable awning called the 'velarium' that could be pulled over the arena to shade spectators from the sun!

questions.question: The Hagia Sophia in Istanbul was originally built as what?
questions.explanation: The Hagia Sophia was originally built as a Christian cathedral in 537 CE. It later became a mosque, then a museum, and is now a mosque again.
questions.funFact: The dome of the Hagia Sophia was so large that people in the 6th century believed it was hung from heaven by a golden chain!

questions.question: The Golden Gate Bridge is a famous red bridge in which American city?
questions.explanation: The Golden Gate Bridge is in San Francisco, California. Despite its name, the bridge is painted 'International Orange,' not gold.
questions.funFact: The Golden Gate Bridge uses enough cable wire to wrap around the Earth three times — about 129,000 kilometres!

questions.question: Mont-Saint-Michel is a medieval island abbey in which country?
questions.explanation: Mont-Saint-Michel is a tidal island in Normandy, France, with a stunning medieval abbey on top. It has been a pilgrimage site for over 1,000 years.
questions.funFact: At high tide, Mont-Saint-Michel is completely surrounded by water and looks like a floating castle!

questions.question: The Terracotta Army is a collection of thousands of clay soldiers found in which country?
questions.explanation: The Terracotta Army was found near Xi'an, China. It was buried with Emperor Qin Shi Huang around 210 BCE to protect him in the afterlife.
questions.funFact: There are over 8,000 terracotta soldiers, and no two faces are exactly alike — each one was individually crafted!

questions.question: What is the Sagrada Familia in Barcelona, Spain?
options: A church that has been under construction since 1882
questions.explanation: The Sagrada Familia is a large church in Barcelona designed by architect Antoni Gaudi. Construction began in 1882 and is still ongoing.
questions.funFact: When finally completed, the Sagrada Familia will have 18 towers, and the tallest will reach 172 metres — making it the tallest church in the world!

questions.question: The Moai statues of Easter Island were carved from what material?
questions.explanation: The Moai statues were carved from volcanic rock, specifically from the Rano Raraku volcano crater on Easter Island.
questions.funFact: The largest Moai ever attempted was over 21 metres tall but was never finished — it still lies in the quarry where it was being carved!

questions.question: The Alhambra is a beautiful palace and fortress in which country?
questions.explanation: The Alhambra is in Granada, Spain. It was built by the Moorish rulers in the 13th and 14th centuries and is known for its stunning Islamic architecture.
questions.funFact: The name Alhambra means 'The Red One' in Arabic because the walls of the fortress glow red in the setting sun!

questions.question: Which ancient Roman building has a famous dome with a hole in the top?
questions.explanation: The Pantheon in Rome has a large circular opening in the top of its dome called an 'oculus.' It was built around 125 CE.
questions.funFact: The Pantheon's dome is still the largest unreinforced concrete dome in the world, nearly 2,000 years after it was built!

questions.question: The Taj Mahal was built by Emperor Shah Jahan. Why did he build it?
options: In memory of his wife Mumtaz Mahal
questions.explanation: Shah Jahan built the Taj Mahal between 1632 and 1653 in memory of his beloved wife Mumtaz Mahal, who died during childbirth.
questions.funFact: More than 20,000 workers and 1,000 elephants helped build the Taj Mahal, and it took about 22 years to finish!

questions.question: Neuschwanstein Castle, which inspired the Disney castle, is in which country?
questions.explanation: Neuschwanstein Castle is in Bavaria, Germany. It was built by King Ludwig II in the 19th century and inspired Sleeping Beauty Castle at Disneyland.
questions.funFact: King Ludwig II only lived in the castle for 172 days before he died, even though it took 17 years to build!

questions.question: What is the Kremlin in Moscow, Russia?
options: A fortified complex that is the seat of Russian government
questions.explanation: The Kremlin is a fortified complex in the heart of Moscow that serves as the official residence of the President of Russia. It contains palaces, cathedrals, and towers.
questions.funFact: The Kremlin walls are over 2 kilometres long and have 20 towers, each with its own unique design!

questions.question: The Great Sphinx of Giza has the body of a lion and the head of what?
questions.explanation: The Great Sphinx has the body of a lion and the head of a human, believed to represent Pharaoh Khafre. It is 73 metres long and 20 metres high.
questions.funFact: The Sphinx was once buried up to its shoulders in sand! It has been dug out and restored many times over thousands of years.

questions.question: The Palace of Versailles is a grand royal palace near which city?
questions.explanation: The Palace of Versailles is near Paris, France. It was the home of French kings including Louis XIV, the Sun King.
questions.funFact: Versailles has 2,300 rooms, including the famous Hall of Mirrors which has 357 mirrors and 20,000 light bulbs!

questions.question: The Blue Mosque, famous for its blue tiles, is in which city?
questions.explanation: The Blue Mosque (Sultan Ahmed Mosque) is in Istanbul, Turkey. It was built between 1609 and 1616 and is famous for its beautiful blue interior tiles.
questions.funFact: The Blue Mosque has over 20,000 handmade ceramic tiles decorating its interior, all painted with blue floral patterns!

questions.question: The Empire State Building was once the tallest building in the world. In which city is it?
questions.explanation: The Empire State Building is in New York City. It was completed in 1931 and stood as the world's tallest building for nearly 40 years.
questions.funFact: The Empire State Building was built in just 410 days — that is just over a year — during the Great Depression!

questions.question: Borobudur, the world's largest Buddhist temple, is located in which country?
questions.explanation: Borobudur is on the island of Java in Indonesia. It was built in the 9th century and has over 2,600 relief panels and 504 Buddha statues.
questions.funFact: Borobudur was buried under volcanic ash and jungle for centuries before it was rediscovered in 1814!

questions.question: The Statue of Unity, the world's tallest statue, is in which country?
questions.explanation: The Statue of Unity is in Gujarat, India. At 182 metres, it is the tallest statue in the world, dedicated to Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel.
questions.funFact: The Statue of Unity is nearly twice as tall as the Statue of Liberty!

questions.question: The Red Fort in Delhi is built from what material?
questions.explanation: The Red Fort is built using red sandstone. It was constructed by Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan in 1639 and served as the main residence of Mughal emperors.
questions.funFact: Every year on Independence Day, the Prime Minister of India hoists the national flag at the Red Fort!

questions.question: The ancient city of Petra was built by which civilization?
questions.explanation: Petra was built by the Nabataeans, an ancient Arab people who controlled important trade routes. The city was carved into sandstone cliffs around 300 BCE.
questions.funFact: Petra has an advanced water system with dams, cisterns, and channels that could collect rainwater in the desert — a marvel of ancient engineering!

questions.question: Which of the original Seven Wonders of the Ancient World still exists today?
questions.explanation: The Great Pyramid of Giza is the only one of the original Seven Wonders of the Ancient World that still stands today.
questions.funFact: The original Seven Wonders list was created by ancient Greek travellers around 200 BCE as a kind of 'tourist guidebook'!

questions.question: The Colosseum in Rome was built using which innovative construction technique?
questions.explanation: The Colosseum was built using Roman concrete, one of the most durable building materials ever invented. It also used travertine stone and iron clamps.
questions.funFact: Roman concrete actually gets stronger over time, unlike modern concrete. Scientists discovered that seawater helps create crystals that reinforce the material!

questions.question: What architectural style is the Taj Mahal an example of?
questions.explanation: The Taj Mahal is a masterpiece of Mughal architecture, which blends Indian, Persian, and Islamic architectural styles.
questions.funFact: Over 28 types of precious and semi-precious stones were used to decorate the Taj Mahal using the pietra dura technique of inlaid gemstones!

questions.question: Machu Picchu was 'rediscovered' by the outside world in which year?
questions.explanation: American historian Hiram Bingham brought Machu Picchu to international attention in 1911, though local people always knew about it.
questions.funFact: Machu Picchu was built without mortar, wheels, or iron tools. The stones fit together so perfectly that you cannot fit a knife blade between them!

questions.question: The Leaning Tower of Pisa began tilting during construction because of what?
options: A design flaw in the foundation
questions.explanation: The tower began to lean because the soil beneath it was too soft on one side. Construction took 199 years (1173-1372) with breaks to let the foundation settle.
questions.funFact: During World War II, the Allies almost demolished the tower because Germans were using it as an observation post, but a retreat order saved it at the last moment!

questions.question: What was the original purpose of the Great Wall of China's watchtowers?
options: Signal communication using smoke and fire
questions.explanation: The watchtowers along the Great Wall were used to send signals using smoke during the day and fire at night to warn of approaching enemies.
questions.funFact: Different numbers of smoke signals meant different things — one column of smoke meant a small enemy force, while five columns meant a massive invasion!

questions.question: The Sydney Opera House was designed by which architect?
questions.explanation: Danish architect Jorn Utzon designed the Sydney Opera House. His design won a competition in 1957 out of 233 entries from around the world.
questions.funFact: The project went massively over budget — originally estimated at $7 million, it ended up costing $102 million and took 16 years to complete!

questions.question: Which architectural order are the columns of the Parthenon built in?
questions.explanation: The Parthenon's outer columns are of the Doric order, the simplest and oldest of the Greek column styles, characterised by plain capitals.
questions.funFact: The columns of the Parthenon are not perfectly straight — they bulge slightly in the middle (called entasis) to create an optical illusion of perfect straightness!

questions.question: The Forbidden City in Beijing contains approximately how many rooms?
questions.explanation: The Forbidden City has approximately 9,000 rooms spread across 980 buildings. It covers 72 hectares and was home to 24 emperors.
questions.funFact: No one was allowed to enter or leave the Forbidden City without the emperor's permission — anyone caught doing so could be executed!

questions.question: Angkor Wat was originally dedicated to which Hindu god?
questions.explanation: Angkor Wat was originally built as a Hindu temple dedicated to Vishnu by King Suryavarman II in the 12th century. It later became a Buddhist temple.
questions.funFact: Angkor Wat's moat is over 5 kilometres long and 190 metres wide. It was designed to represent the cosmic ocean in Hindu mythology!

questions.question: What engineering challenge did the builders of the Burj Khalifa face in the desert?
options: Building on soft sandy soil with a high water table
questions.explanation: The Burj Khalifa's foundation sits on soft desert soil, requiring 192 piles driven 50 metres deep into the ground, plus a massive concrete mat to support the tower.
questions.funFact: The Burj Khalifa has 163 floors and 57 elevators. One of the elevators is the fastest in the world, travelling at 10 metres per second!

questions.question: The Christ the Redeemer statue in Brazil was built using which material?
questions.explanation: Christ the Redeemer is made of reinforced concrete and covered with approximately 6 million soapstone tiles. It was completed in 1931.
questions.funFact: Lightning strikes the statue about 3 to 5 times per year! In 2014, a lightning bolt chipped off the tip of one of its fingers.

questions.question: Which of these UNESCO World Heritage Sites is the most recently inscribed?
questions.explanation: The Burj Khalifa is not a UNESCO World Heritage Site. While impressive, UNESCO sites are typically chosen for cultural or natural significance rather than modern height records.
questions.funFact: There are over 1,100 UNESCO World Heritage Sites across 167 countries. Italy has the most with 59 sites!

questions.question: Stonehenge's massive stones were transported from how far away?
questions.explanation: The smaller 'bluestones' at Stonehenge were transported from the Preseli Hills in Wales, about 240 kilometres away. How ancient people moved them remains debated.
questions.funFact: The larger sarsen stones weigh up to 25 tonnes each, and scientists now believe they came from a site called West Woods, about 25 kilometres away!

questions.question: The Palace of Versailles' Hall of Mirrors was significant because of what historical event?
options: Signing of the Treaty of Versailles after World War I
questions.explanation: The Treaty of Versailles, which ended World War I, was signed in the Hall of Mirrors on June 28, 1919, making it one of history's most important rooms.
questions.funFact: The Hall of Mirrors contains 357 mirrors that reflect the light from 20,000 candles and 150 chandeliers, creating a dazzling display!

questions.question: What makes Gaudi's Sagrada Familia unique in terms of its construction timeline?
options: It was built in just one year
It has been under construction for over 140 years
It was completed before Gaudi designed it
questions.explanation: Construction of the Sagrada Familia began in 1882 and is expected to be completed around 2026. Gaudi knew he would not live to see it finished.
questions.funFact: When Gaudi died in 1926, only about 25% of the church was complete. He spent the last 15 years of his life working on nothing else!

questions.question: The Terracotta Army was discovered accidentally by whom?
questions.explanation: The Terracotta Army was discovered in 1974 by local farmers who were digging a well near Xi'an, China. It is considered one of the greatest archaeological finds ever.
questions.funFact: Each terracotta warrior originally held real bronze weapons. Many of these weapons were still sharp after being buried for over 2,000 years!

questions.question: The Alhambra in Spain is an outstanding example of which architectural tradition?
questions.explanation: The Alhambra is a masterpiece of Islamic Moorish architecture, featuring intricate geometric patterns, arabesques, muqarnas (honeycomb vaulting), and calligraphy.
questions.funFact: The Alhambra's walls are decorated with over 10,000 inscriptions in Arabic, many of which are poems praising the beauty of the palace!

questions.question: What is special about the construction of the Pantheon's dome in Rome?
options: It is the largest unreinforced concrete dome ever built
It was built in a single day
questions.explanation: The Pantheon's dome, built around 125 CE, remains the world's largest unreinforced concrete dome at 43.3 metres in diameter — a feat not matched for 1,300 years.
questions.funFact: The dome gets thinner and lighter toward the top, and the concrete mix changes from heavy basalt at the bottom to lightweight pumice at the top!

questions.question: The Konark Sun Temple in Odisha, India, is designed in the shape of what?
questions.explanation: The Konark Sun Temple is designed as a gigantic chariot with 24 elaborately carved stone wheels, pulled by seven horses, dedicated to the Sun god Surya.
questions.funFact: The 24 wheels of the Konark Sun Temple actually work as sundials — you can tell the exact time of day using the shadow they cast!

questions.question: The Hagia Sophia has served as a cathedral, a mosque, and a museum. In which year was it first built?
questions.explanation: The Hagia Sophia was completed in 537 CE under Byzantine Emperor Justinian I. For nearly 1,000 years, it was the largest cathedral in the world.
questions.funFact: When Emperor Justinian first entered the completed Hagia Sophia, he reportedly exclaimed, 'Solomon, I have outdone thee!' referring to the biblical Temple of Solomon.

questions.question: Which of these pairs correctly matches a monument with its primary building material?
options: Machu Picchu — granite blocks without mortar
questions.explanation: Machu Picchu was built using granite blocks fitted together without mortar using a technique called ashlar. The stones fit so precisely they have survived earthquakes.
questions.funFact: Machu Picchu sits on two fault lines, and the Incas' dry-stone technique actually allows the walls to bounce during earthquakes and resettle!

questions.question: The Statue of Liberty's crown has how many rays, and what do they represent?
options: 7 rays for the 7 continents and 7 seas
questions.explanation: The crown has 7 rays representing the seven continents and seven seas of the world, symbolising liberty spreading across the globe.
questions.funFact: Visitors must climb 354 steps to reach the crown of the Statue of Liberty — that is equivalent to climbing a 20-storey building!

questions.question: Which monument was intentionally designed so that during equinoxes, the shadows create a serpent effect on its staircase?
questions.explanation: El Castillo (Temple of Kukulcan) at Chichen Itza was designed so that during the spring and autumn equinoxes, shadows create the illusion of a serpent descending the pyramid.
questions.funFact: The pyramid has 365 steps in total — one for each day of the year — showing the Maya's incredible knowledge of astronomy and mathematics!
